lesson_title: Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.back: Mersenne Twister with a period of 2^19937-1. It's a high-quality pseudo-random number generator.
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.front: What is the difference between std::mt19937 and std::mt19937_64?
cards.back: std::mt19937 generates 32-bit random numbers. std::mt19937_64 generates 64-bit random numbers.
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.text: To get a truly random seed from the OS, use {{c1::std::random_device}}. Call it with {{c2::rd()}} to get a random value.
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.question: How do you properly initialize a Mersenne Twister with a random seed?
cards.code:
{{c1::std::random_device rd;}}
{{c2::std::mt19937 mt{rd()};}} // seed with random value
cards.explanation: Create a random_device, then use it to seed the mt19937 engine. The rd() call gets a random value from the OS.
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.front: Why should the Mersenne Twister engine be declared static in a function?
cards.back: So it persists between function calls and maintains its state. This ensures you get different random numbers each call, not the same sequence.
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.text:
To generate a random integer between min and max (inclusive):
{{c1::std::uniform_int_distribution dist{min, max};}}
{{c2::int value = dist(mt);}}
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.front: Is the upper bound of std::uniform_int_distribution inclusive or exclusive?
cards.back: Inclusive. If you create dist{1, 6}, both 1 and 6 can be generated (like a dice roll).
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.concept: Complete pattern for random number generation function
cards.explanation:
The recommended pattern for a random number function:
  • Make engine static so it persists
  • Seed once with std::random_device
  • Create distribution for desired range
  • Call distribution with engine to generate
  • Return the random value
This gives high-quality randomness with different values each call.
examples:
int random(int min, int max)
{
    static std::mt19937 mt{std::random_device{}()};
    std::uniform_int_distribution die{min, max};
    return die(mt);
}
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.question: What's wrong with this random number function?
cards.code:
int random(int min, int max)
{
    std::mt19937 mt{std::random_device{}()};
    std::uniform_int_distribution die{min, max};
    return die(mt);
}
cards.answer: The engine mt is recreated and reseeded on every call, which is slow and may produce similar sequences.
cards.explanation: Without static, the engine is created new each call. This is inefficient and defeats the purpose of a PRNG's state. Make it static.
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.front: Why shouldn't you use std::random_device directly for all random number generation?
cards.back: It can be slow (accesses hardware/OS resources), may have limited entropy, and on some systems might not actually be random. Use it only for seeding.
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.text:
The common idiom to seed mt19937 inline:
{{c1::std::mt19937 mt{std::random_device{}()};}}
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister

cards.front: How can you make random number generation reproducible (same sequence each run) for debugging?
cards.back:
Seed the Mersenne Twister with a fixed value instead of std::random_device:
std::mt19937 mt{12345}; // fixed seed
cards.source: 8.14 - Generating random numbers using Mersenne Twister
